title: 25-6060 - United States v. Brown
text:
Appellate Case: 25-6060 Document: 31-1 Date Filed: 04/07/2026 Page: 1
FILED
United States Court of Appeals
Tenth Circuit
UNITED STATES COURT OF APPEALS
April 7, 2026
FOR THE TENTH CIRCUIT
_______________________________________
Christopher M. Wolpert
Clerk of Court
UNITED STATES OF AMERICA,
Plaintiff - Appellee,
v. No. 25-6060
(D.C. No. 5:20-CR-00277-G-1)
OSHAY SHADON BROWN, (W.D. Okla.)
Defendant - Appellant.
_______________________________________
ORDER
_______________________________________
Before BACHARACH, MORITZ, and ROSSMAN, Circuit Judges.
_______________________________________
Mr. Oshay Shadon Brown appeals the revocation of his supervised
release and the resulting 24-month sentence. His appointed attorney has
moved to withdraw, stating that all of Mr. Brown’s grounds to appeal
would be frivolous. See Anders v. California, 386 U.S. 738 (1967).
Mr. Brown did not respond, and we agree with his counsel about the
frivolousness of any potential grounds to appeal.
The district court revoked supervised release based on Mr. Brown’s
• assault and battery against his girlfriend, Ms. Desirae Burton,
under tribal law and
• violation of a no-contact order by communicating with the
girlfriend.

Appellate Case: 25-6060 Document: 31-1 Date Filed: 04/07/2026 Page: 2
The government presented three forms of proof about the assault and
battery. First, an eyewitness testified that he had seen Mr. Brown punch
Ms. Burton. Second, officers testified that Ms. Burton had identified
Mr. Brown as the assailant and had suffered visible injuries consistent with
an assault. Third, photographs depicted the injuries.
At the hearing itself, Ms. Burton denied that she had been hit. But
the district court credited her contemporaneous statements and the
eyewitness testimony.
The government also claimed that Mr. Brown had violated a no-
contact order issued in tribal court. For this claim, the government
presented evidence of the order and Mr. Brown’s calls to the girlfriend.
Ms. Burton admitted that the calls had taken place while the order was in
effect.
On appeal, Mr. Brown’s counsel identifies two possible appellate
arguments:
1. The evidence was insufficient to support revocation.
2. The victim’s inconsistent statements rendered her unreliable.
These arguments would be groundless. For revocation of supervised
release, the district court needed only to find a violation of the conditions
by a preponderance of the evidence. United States v. Williams, 106 F.4th
1040, 1047 (10th Cir. 2024). We review factual findings for clear error and
defer to credibility determinations. United States v. Handley, 678 F.3d
2

Appellate Case: 25-6060 Document: 31-1 Date Filed: 04/07/2026 Page: 3
1185, 1188 (10th Cir. 2012); see also United States v. Jordan, 806 F.3d
1244, 1252 (10th Cir. 2015) (stating that we defer to a district court’s
factual findings and give particular deference to findings on credibility).
For the first finding, an appellate court couldn’t possibly disturb the
findings given eyewitness testimony, testimony about Ms. Burton’s
contemporaneous statements, and photographs of the injuries.
For the second finding, an appellate court couldn’t possibly disturb
the finding of a violation in light of (1) the call logs and (2) Ms. Burton’s
testimony that the calls had taken place while a non- contact order was in
place.
Nor is the sentence subject to a potential challenge. The sentence fell
within the ranges under the guideline s and governing statute, see
U.S.S.G. § 7B1.4(b)(1); 18 U.S.C. § 3583(e)(3), and the record doesn’t
suggest any possible basis to question the reasonableness of the sentence.
Disposition
We conclude that Mr. Brown’s potential appellate arguments would
be frivolous. So we grant counsel’s motion to withdraw and dismiss the
appeal.
Entered for the Court
Robert E. Bacharach
Circuit Judge
3
